params.text:
Communication Skills in Care

Communication is the foundation of good care. Everything we do — building trust, keeping people safe, respecting choices, working as a team — depends on communicating well. In this module you'll explore what good communication looks like in a care setting and practise it through short interactive activities.

By the end you will be able to:

  • Describe the communication cycle and the main types of communication
  • Use active-listening techniques with the people you support
  • Recognise and reduce barriers to communication
  • Adapt your communication to individual needs, including sensory and cognitive needs
  • Understand your duty to record and report communication accurately

The communication cycle

Communication is a two-way process. A sender forms a message and sends it; a receiver takes it in, interprets it, and sends feedback. At every step the message can be strengthened or distorted.

  1. Sender — decides what to communicate and how.
  2. Message — the words, tone, and body language used.
  3. Receiver — hears, sees, and interprets the message.
  4. Feedback — shows whether the message was understood.

Checking feedback — "Have I understood you correctly?" — is what turns talking at someone into communicating with them.

params.title: Types of communication — tap each card to flip it
params.description: Tap a card to reveal what each type of communication means in care.

dialogs.answer: The words we speak — including tone, pace, and volume. Speak clearly, avoid jargon, and check understanding.
Body language, facial expression, eye contact, gestures, and touch. Often says more than words — around half of a message is non-verbal.
Care plans, daily notes, handovers, texts and emails. Must be clear, factual, timely, and legible — others rely on it.
Pictures, symbols, objects of reference, Makaton, hearing aids and communication boards that help a person express themselves.

params.text:
Active listening — the SOLER approach

Listening is an active skill, not just staying quiet. A simple way to remember good listening posture is SOLER:

  • S — Sit or face the person squarely
  • O — Keep an open posture (don't fold your arms)
  • L — Lean slightly towards the person
  • E — Maintain comfortable eye contact
  • R — Stay relaxed

Add verbal signs you're listening — nodding, "I see", reflecting back what you heard — and give the person time. Never rush or finish their sentences.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these communication principles.
params.textField:
Communication is a *two-way* process between a sender and a receiver.
Around half of any message is carried through *non-verbal* signals such as body language.
To show you are listening, you should *reflect* back what the person has said.
Always *check* understanding rather than assuming the message was received.
Written records must be clear, factual and *timely*.

params.text:
Barriers to communication

Many things can get in the way of a clear message. Being aware of them helps you reduce them:

  • Physical — noise, poor lighting, distance, PPE covering your face.
  • Sensory — hearing or sight loss, or a broken hearing aid.
  • Cognitive — dementia, learning disability, confusion or medication effects.
  • Language — English as an additional language, jargon, or complex words.
  • Emotional — pain, fear, anxiety, or feeling rushed.

Reduce barriers: cut background noise, face the person, use short sentences, allow time, and use aids the person relies on.

params.question: Mrs Patel wears a hearing aid. You notice she keeps saying "pardon?" and looks anxious. What is the best first response?

answers.text: Check her hearing aid is switched on and working, reduce background noise, and face her while speaking clearly.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct — remove the barrier and adapt how you communicate.

answers.text: Speak much louder and more slowly to everyone in the room.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Shouting distorts speech and can feel undignified.

answers.text: Write everything down instead, as talking clearly isn't working.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Writing may help later, but first check the aid and the environment.

answers.text: Carry on as normal — she will ask again if she needs to.

tipsAndFeedback.notChosenFeedback: Ignoring the barrier risks her safety and dignity.

params.text:
Communicating with individual needs

Person-centred communication means adapting to the individual — never a one-size-fits-all script.

  • Dementia: keep it calm and simple, one idea at a time, use names not "he/she", and go with their reality rather than correcting.
  • Sight loss: introduce yourself by name, say when you're leaving, describe what's happening.
  • Hearing loss: face the person, don't cover your mouth, use gestures and written notes, check aids.
  • Learning disability: use easy-read, pictures or Makaton, and allow plenty of time.

params.question: It is good practice to correct a person living with dementia every time their account of events differs from reality.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite — repeatedly correcting can cause distress. Meet the person in their reality and reassure them.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — avoid arguing with their reality; reassure and redirect instead.

params.text:
Recording & reporting

Communication doesn't end when a conversation does. What you record and hand over keeps the person safe and their care consistent.

  • Write facts, not opinions — what you saw, heard and did.
  • Record promptly and sign/date every entry.
  • Report changes, concerns and refusals to the right person straight away.
  • Keep information confidential and share only on a need-to-know basis.

Key takeaway: good communication is two-way, adapted to the individual, and always followed by clear, factual recording.
